reasoning: The Lean signature universally quantifies over finite groups G and primes p, q : ℕ, asserts |G| = p^2*q via card G, and concludes ¬IsSimpleGroup G (encoded as IsSimpleGroup G → False). This matches the informal claim "no group of order p^2q (p,q prime) is simple" exactly: same hypotheses (finite group, p and q prime, order equal to p^2q), same conclusion (not simple), same quantifier structure (universal over all such groups). The informal phrasing does not explicitly require p ≠ q, and even the degenerate case p = q (order p^3) satisfies the claim, so no hidden drift in hypotheses occurs. Prime on ℕ correctly captures primality. Thus the Lean statement is logically equivalent to the informal statement.
